author: Christina Morales
commentBody: The lamb was delicious, great flavor combo.  I sauteed all of the veggies, including the onions and chilis, before adding the lamb, because I didn't like the idea of nearly raw onions and jalepeno.  The result was less browning, but a lovely blend of pungent flavors after I added the cumin and chili powder.   The "buns", however, were a total dud.  Dense hockey pucks that were raw inside and inedible.  I would recommend using Meera Sodha's recipe for naan on this website; its amazing.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: For the spice mixture Chile powder I used gochugaru and cayenne. I also pre-marinated the lamb in some baking soda, soy sauce, msg, and shaoxing (small amounts of all for ~2hrs). Only other sub was jalapeno peppers. The meat came out wonderfully spiced, tender, and moist. Definitely a keeper. I'm not sure what, but I think the buns could benefit from a topping, something light, maybe an acid based slaw to add some crunch and freshness.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
Thought the recipe was way too heavy on the ground cumin, to the extent that I couldn't really identify the meat beneath all that cumin.  If I were to make these again, I'd reduce the cumin by half.  

Also, I used normal-sized burger buns in lieu of making the buns.  I found that the amount of cooked meat was way too much for normal burger buns.  I could have easily made twice as many sandwiches.

Also, not being a fan of raw hot peppers or raw red onion, I cooked both with the lamb.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
Be sure the wok/skillet is VERY hot, as the lamb will release a good bit of fat.  I made mine in a wok and had a hard time getting the lamb to brown, so I finished it under a broiler for some texture.  Next time I'll use a large skillet with more surface area.  

That said, the flavors were great (as always, lamb needs a generous amount of salt) and we loved it paired with a simple shirazi salad (not Asian of course, but still a nice complement).

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: The best sandwich is the simplist one. A few years ago a neighbor gifted us a couple of large ripe heirloom tomatoes. I placed a few slices on white bread, added a little salt, dabbed a decent smear of mayonniase and took a bite. Wow! It was a revelation that something so simple can taste so good. Tried sprinkling a little parmesan but it killed the pureness of the tomato. I truly believe a well constructed tomato sandwich can stand up to a cucumber sandwich any day of the week.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody: Placing tomatoes directly against the bread changes its texture by making it moist. The riper the tomato, the wetter the bread will become, especially if the sandwich is packed to be eaten later. The solution is to place dry ingredients against the bread slices. Therefore, the order in this sandwich should be: bread-halloumi-tomato-arugula-bread.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Halloumi, Arugula and Tomato Sandwiches Recipe
commentBody:
I made this sandwich using all the ingredients and following the directions exactly, with the following exceptions only:
- Light Duke’s mayo (don’t hate lol)
- Power greens subbed for arugula (I had them on hand)
- I added red pepper flakes to the Greek dressing for the salad (I love heat)
- I used pickled red onions because yes

I thought it was delicious. 

Would I maybe use less mayo next time? Sure. Might I stack it differently next time? Quite possibly. Add pickles? Yum. Is less often more? Yup. And so on. 

This sandwich, as the recipe dictates and as I bowdlerized it, was fabulous.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: I’m not a baker, because I don’t do exact measurements or even exact ingredients. Still, this recipe turned out great! Fluffy texture just out of the oven, hardening a bit (enough to cut cleanly) after 5-10 minutes. Here are the substitutions I made: used a 9"x9" pan (what I had), used unsifted fine rice flour (because gluten free), used raw sugar (what I had), and used Torani caramel sauce from the coffee-flavorings area of my grocery store. Very tasty, easy, fast.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: The sauce this produces is basically what we call a pipian verde in Mexico. If you substitute chilies poblano for the bell peppers and use a comal or heavy fry pan for browning the tomatillos, onion, chilies, and garlic, you are making a simple pipian with this recipe and I like it very much. I substitute a good quality tahini for the roasted and ground sesame seeds and it works well.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
This is outstanding. Made as is with just a few method changes to simplify and reduce pan usage. 
Season and brown the thighs in the Dutch oven (because why wouldn’t you?!?), remove them and immediately fry the garlic in the same pot. Deglaze with white wine and add the broiled veg and stock. Return the chicken with juices and off you go. 
Also, add the ground nut mixture to the blender to puree with the broth, veg, and cilantro. 
Comforting, colorful, delicious.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: Very tasty, which I earned over the hours of making this stew. It’s a pain, to be honest. I won’t make it again unless I have time to break down the steps—maybe do the broiling and seed toasting and grinding the night before, make the stew up to pureeing in the morning (so it can cool before pureeing,) then reheating and serving that evening. The one upside is that we have stew to eat for the next couple of nights.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: Culantro is a great substitute for cilantro. Harder to find, but the outcome of the taste is the same. We use it as a work-around for a coriander allergy. Not sure if it'll work for folks who think cilantro tastes like soap but it's worth a try. Most good "farmers" markets have it. We find it at a big Asian market near our house that has a great variety of fresh herbs.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: I am glad I made this recipe but it was a lot of work.  Two of us tag-teamed this recipe and it took us over 2.5 hours of constant work, but we had fun.  We fed this to 12 people, including 6 kids, and everyone liked it and a couple people loved it, but I still feel like the recipe isn't quite there.  As if there is one spice missing that would push it over the top, but I don't know what it is missing.  We tried lime juice and maybe that helped. 6.5 cups of stock yielded a very thick stew.

commentBody: Terrific! Very versatile and easily adjusted if required. I realized (to late) that the pepper I used was a "mystery pepper" from my garden, which literally set my whole face (and the dish) on fire. Absolutely lovely dish! Will make this again!

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: This recipe looked delicious and I wanted to cook it immediately but didn’t have all the ingredients so I improvised with what I had. I was cooking for 2 so made half a recipe. I used fajita chicken, which I browned with the onions and a little cumin and cinnamon. I didn’t have jalapeños or green peppers so I subbed a can of hatch chilis, and didn’t have tomatillos but had a can of roasted diced green tomatoes, so I added the tomatoes and chilis after slightly cooking the fajita strips. I only added 1 cup of broth which seemed like enough. The cilantro and seed mixture really added something special! I didn’t blend the soup, just ate it as a stew. It was delicious, but I look forward to making it as written.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Suspiro a la Limeña (Caramel Pudding With Port Meringue) Recipe
commentBody:
First of all, the recipe is clear as day - 8 yolks and 2 egg whites.

You can confirm this with some general knowledge of cookery. 8 or 10 whites would make an insane amount of meringue. And egg whites have no place in a custard. 

Furthermore, the egg whites are not raw - they are turned into an Italian meringue with the addition of the hot syrup.

recipeTitle: Suspiro a la Limeña (Caramel Pudding With Port Meringue) Recipe
commentBody: Two of my guy’s favorite things are dulce de leche and port. So when I spotted this a few days ago and knew I had to make it. I didn’t have any trouble with the custard coming together, though I did cook the milks at a higher temperature than called for. Stirred with a rubber spatula near constantly but it reached the right consistency in about 30 minutes. Custard set up beautifully and the port meringue was lovely. Everything was delicious, though it is REALLY rich so could only eat a few bites. But it’s like eating spoonfuls of caramel. Super decadent.

The custard was delicious, but for me the meringue felt like it came out too think and didnt have the flavour i expected given i used a port that I love. 

If I tried it again I probably wouldn't use a ruby port, but I doubt that I will because texturally I didn't really enjoy the meringue and custard combo. 

As others said, took way longer than 30 minutes to come together, closer to 45-50 for the custard and 30 for the port syrup.

I happen not to like the taste of raw egg white meringue, so I’d change it anyway. But it really depends on the situation in your area. I have lived on the planet for over half a century and have literally never encountered a bad egg from the store.

The point though is that people eat poached eggs all the time. I’m a food safety geek but I have to confess to loving them.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: I am seeing a lot of questions and comments about the cheese. I am not Paraguayan but lived in rural Paraguay for about two years. Queso paraguayo is a fresh farmer’s cheese, but it is very different than Mexican queso fresco in both taste and consistency. The cheese itself changes a lot as it ages. You can put any cheese in here you want - it’s cornbread! But I think something stretchy and melty like mozzarella or Muenster will get you a little closer to the real thing than queso fresco.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
The corn-meal traditional in most Central/South American regions is made with what we call "hominy," (corn kernels boiled with calcium hydroxide, washed and dried before hulling and grinding).  This treatment is called "nixtamalization," and prevents dietary diseases such as kwashiorkor and pellagra as well as neutralizing some fungal toxins.  

In Southern US - where pellagra was a serious problem into the 20th century - wheat flour was added to prevent the disease.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
After researching what precooked cornmeal is, I finally read the recipe ingredients completely. Other than this being heavier on eggs and milk, it seems to be what I've always known as Mexican cornbread...essentially cornbread with corn, onions, cheese and maybe peppers added.

Years ago, at Golden Corral of all places, I think I may have had Sopa. They had something they called a cornbread casserole that I remember as being much like this recipe. It was good!

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: I just made this and it's delicious - fluffy and savory yet a bit sweet, and very light on the corn-y taste. When I make it again, I'll add another cup of the corn ingredient. I made it my main dish along with some charred brussel sprouts and it was plenty fulfilling; I didn't miss the grilled meat component at all. I didn't add enough salt (I try to err on the lighter side) and it may have been a mistake, it definitely needed the full 1 1/2 teaspoons). I'll give this an A+!

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: Reminds me of an easier recipe I learned from my brother while visiting him when he was in the Air Force.  1 Box of Jiffy cornbread mix - mixed per box directions.  AND 1 8-oz can of creamed corn, 1 4-oz can of diced green chiles, and maybe a cup of grated cheddar.  Bake using the box instructions, but will likely need more time due to the added wet ingredients.  Very moist, requiring fork or spoon, and delicious.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: PAN corn flour (noticed on the label that it’s no longer made in Venezuela but in Texas!) is a staple in our house so was glad to see something besides arepas (and almojábanas) to use it for. The recipe turned out perfectly - light, moist and delicious…begging for some salted butter on top. Maybe cooks here were expecting more of an American cornbread type thing…not the same. But yes, I’d take out all those appliances and make it again!

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
For all the people asking to reduce key ingredients like oil or make it healthier, I suggest either choose a completely different recipe (this one isn't really a true cornbread - as another user mentions, it is more like a souffle or quiche), or just make half this recipe and serve smaller portions.
My tip, which I hope won't offend, is that I plan to try making this recipe again adding either some salt + cayenne, or maybe Old Bay, to make it a bit more punchy/savory.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
JALAPEÑO CORN CASSEROLE
1    stick butter 
1    large onion-chopped
1-2 chopped jalapeños 
3    eggs
1    large can cream style corn 
2    large cans whole kernel corn & juice
2     boxes Jiffy Corn Bread Mix
2     cups grated cheddar cheese
Sauté onions & jalapeños in butter.
In bowl, mix beaten eggs, corn bread mix, creamed corn & whole kernel corn with its juice.
Lightly grease a 9 X13 in. pan. Pour corn bread mix into pan
Spoon onions & jalapeños & cheese on top. Bake 375 for 45 mins.Yum!

commentBody: This cornbread was delicious. I made a few adjustments based on what I had on hand- cheddar cheese worked great, and I used 1/2 buttermilk and 1/2 2% milk. I found the PAN cornnmeal at a local Mexican grocery- It's got fine texture and a beautiful yellow color. I'm going to try it for "regular" cornbread.

commentBody: On this, I would also add that there are massive regional differences in how the Hallaca is made in Venezuela. In Margarita they put aubergine. It is really versatile.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: The author of the first great reference book of Venezuelan cuisine, Don Armando Scannone, once said that "The tamale is a case of corn with a little filling. The hallaca is an excellent filling with a very fine case of corn". This perfectly defines what this emblematic Venezuelan dish consists of, not only gastronomically, but also culturally and societally. Its recipe is an excellent starting point for all the varieties that exist in the country.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
When I suddenly found myself living in rural Pennsylvania as a young boy in the pre-Goya 1960’s having moved from Caracas what I missed most were Hallacas at Christmas and quanabana. 
This recipe is incorrect. A typical Hallaca does not have garbanzos, potatoes, and carrots and Is missing many of the sweet/salty combinations which make up the basis of Venezuelan cuisine. This hallaca is more of an Andean version typical of a Columbian pastele without the annato tinted masa.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: This takes me back to my childhood in the 1960s when my Puerto Rican mother commandeered our suburban Maryland kitchen during the holidays making pasteles! Similar to the Venezuelan dish, but with a base of ground green plantains instead of corn, it was us kids’ job to peel the green plantains and feed them into a hand grinder to be later flavored with annatto oil. Mom would make a hundred at a time and the kitchen would be buzzing for two days. Thanks for bringing back happy memories!!

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
Okay, let’s be honest: this is not going to fool your abuela and why would you want to fool her anyway??? Tell her you have a delicious vegetarian dish that reminds you of her pernil (but of course not as good as hers!) and let her enjoy it in its own right! Because it’s delicious. And every member of my family inhaled it. And no, it isn’t pork. But it’s great.

Notes: i used the red goya sofrito because that’s what my grocery store had. It’s fine, homemade would be better. Made my own adobo.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: I was looking for something “eggnog-ish” (creamy, sweet, thick and boozy) that was quick and easy to make. I followed the recipe except I used only 1 can of condensed milk and a whole can of coconut milk, and omitted lime zest because I didn’t have any (I will infuse and strain later when I get some). Anyhoo, I cannot imagine using 2 cans of sweetened condensed milk because holy moly, this is sweet! But it’s definitely a keeper. Can’t wait to add it to my coffee!

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: The original recipe, as transcribed in 1993 by famed mixologist Calvin Broadus, calls for Seagram's Gin and an indo accompaniment, with each drinker providing his own cup and (hopefully) chipping in on the alcohol purchase.  A fancier version, often attributed to Andre Young, uses Tanqueray and is typically accompanied by the bubonic chronic.  Both recipes are delicious, but I find that the first produces a more contemplative, "laid back" effect, while the second poses greater challenges.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
Lovely drink I have often enjoyed under a different name, the venerable Bronx cocktail.  This recipe is essentially the same as a Bronx.  Here is the Food Network recipe:

In a cocktail shaker, combine 1 ounce gin, 2 ounces orange juice and 1/2 ounce each sweet and dry vermouth. Shake with ice for 30 seconds, then strain into a cocktail glass; garnish with an orange twist.

commentBody: Oddly I thought the white vermouth would add a little “salt” taste to it but I found it distracting. commentBody: For cooks asking about source for panela (brown sugar cane - piloncillo or dulce de atado), Google or Amazon both come in handy if you're stumped. author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Ayote en Miel (Squash With Spiced Syrup) Recipe
commentBody: I made this today, and it is delicious. However, I am baffled by the timing in the recipe. After dissolving the panela (I used jaggery, same-same) I added butternut squash and cooked, covered, for 45 minutes. At that point it was almost falling apart and clearly if I had left it in the syrup much longer it would have dissolved into soup. So I took it out, placing the pieces in a ceramic lasagna pan. Then I boiled the syrup down to a lovely thick caramel-like sauce, and poured it over the squash pieces. It’s great, I’m delighted, everything’s fine. But no way could it have survived 2 hours of simmering. And a Cinderella Pumpkin wouldn’t have lasted any longer. So I’m mystified. Anyway, 5 stars, 2 thumbs up, go for it!

commentBody: It is a lot of sugar, but you can try it with less. I have made something similar called simply calabasa. It also uses piloncillo, and you can find it easily, but it looks rough and crude. I bought mine wrapped in a corn leaf. Super sweet, but filled with the bounty of the harvest. It's probably more of a Northern harvest in Mexico. I learned this recipe from my mother-in-law, and her heritage points in that direction. She adds other small fruits and nuts, boiling down the calabasa.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: I've made lots of djon-djon rice, introduced by my Haitian neighbor. There's no substitute for djon-djon. Woodsy and floral. Unique fragrance and flavor. I've tried other types (wood-ear, shiitake, etc.) -- not even in the ballpark. I use pigeon peas not lima beans, and keep blended epis frozen. Maggi djon-djon cubes for mushrooms are like top-ramen chicken packets for homemade stock. Or just slightly better than that. The mushroom's central to the dish. Support Haitian self-determination.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Pasta con Palta (Creamy Avocado Pesto Pasta) Recipe
commentBody: Hmm - I've been married into a Chilean family for 25 years. They are predictable fanatics when it comes to "palta," but no one has ever heard of putting it on pasta. One thing I can say is that if this were made in Chile it would definitely have lemon mixed into the palta. The addition of tomatoes would be very authentic as well. And then they'd skip the pasta and smear the whole thing on bread with a nice bottle of red, which is very Chilean.

recipeTitle: Pasta con Palta (Creamy Avocado Pesto Pasta) Recipe
commentBody: Reading the recipe it reminded me very much of dishes that I had while in Chile - simple, but delicious. However, it relies on all the ingredients being exceptionally fresh and of great quality. Sadly, grocery store avocados - or at least the ones I can get my hands on in Virginia - are never going to fit that bill. I made as written and discovered it to be very bland. So I added some sun dried tomatoes and some cojito cheese to punch it up.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Pasta con Palta (Creamy Avocado Pesto Pasta) Recipe
commentBody: I recently started to buy avocados in bulk and freeze them.  I let them ripen, cut in quarters to easily and safely pit them, drop the quarters in lemon juice that I then drain off them and place them in freezer bags, 2 avocados/bag.  When they thaw, they are a bit mushy, perfect for this recipe, a rough guacamole, or mashed as a sandwich garnish.  My new fav hack and my new fav recipe.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Pasta con Palta (Creamy Avocado Pesto Pasta) Recipe
commentBody:
This was way better than I thought it was going to be and so simple. I added the juice and zest of a lemon to it and make sure you use plenty of black pepper. The walnut was really nice to add a little complexity and I garnished with the lemon zest and some rough chopped walnut for a little crunch. 

I went heavier on the avocado ratio to make the sauce extra thick compared to the picture on this recipe showed. This would likely be really nice with a piece of cooked chicken breast and/or some halved/quartered cherry tomatoes as well. 

Worked awesome in my immersion blender processor attachment. No mess and crazy quick to make.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Arroz con Maiz y Crema (Cheesy Rice Casserole With Corn) Recipe
commentBody: This is a wonderful recipe. It can scale to serve 2-20. I first had a variation of this 55y ago; this version is great. Substitutions are simple. Precook the rice. If it's leftover and dry, adjust the mayo and sour cream. Whatever mild peppers you have will work (jarred red peppers or pimientos, roasted poblanos, or canned green chiles). Grated carrots add flavor, color, nutrition; corn too. Blends of cheddar & Jack cheese or Parmesan & goat add new flavors (haven't tried blue or swiss cheeses).

Rub raw garlic on the toast.

Grind a bit o pepper.
Cilantro/parsley chopped coarse 
Halve a tomato and squeeze out a juicy tomato over greens.FUN!! (Eat skin and hard pulp on the side).
Squeeze of lemon.
Drizzle Evoo
Sprinkle Maldon salt

Bottle of Viogner is nice with this Umami goodness

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: When I lived in Spain 40 years ago, I learned to make ham, tuna, chorizo or cod croquettes for my family from an old pueblo woman in Campo Real.  There were no refrigerator or freezer pauses.  Just make the dough quite stiff, then grab a small handful, roll it, coat with bread crumbs and deep fry.  I also learned how to make empanadillas with some of the same proteins, summered in homemade pesto.  To this day, neither me nor anyone I’ve ever made these for have had one better.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
This is a go-to recipe. I live in Europe and we have no chicken parmesan and I miss it from the Italian store I used to live across from in New Haven.

I make some modifications:
I use Ritz crackers for the crumb instead of the bread crumbs/panko. They have more oil and brown better.
I add a tablespoon or two of mayo with the egg, it helps protect the chicken better.
I add a lot of garlic powder and double the Italian seasoning.
I sometimes salt the chicken breasts a few hrs before cooking

I had some gingerbread cookies in the freezer  from Christmas. Pulsed them in the FP with some melted butter. Baked in regular oven @ 375 12 mins. I wanted a crispy crust. Continued with the recipe, as written. Added a little vanilla bean paste along with the vanilla. I wanted those little specks of vanilla bean. Made a sling out of foil to ease lifting the cheesecake out of AF. My 6” springform pan fit perfectly in the AF. Baked @ 300 for 30. Will make again!!

commentBody: Utterly delicious. Made this last night for a dinner party and the guests raved! commentBody: I'm not a big fan of cheesecake but decided to make this one for my wife's Valentine. I followed the recipe ingredients exactly but did a lot of extra beating to fully emulsify the batter.  After baking 25 minutes @ 300 the top crust was browned but the "jiggle"wasn't slight so I reduced the temp to 275 and kept checking and jiggling until the crust was very well browned and the jiggle had lessened. Following directions, and with a thickened blueberry compote (wife's), it was delightful! 5*

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: Amazing how well this worked!  I wanted to lighten up the calories, so used the America's Test Kitchen "The Best Light Recipe" cheesecake recipe, which includes light cream cheese, drained and whipped cottage cheese, and drained yogurt (labneh).  Further healthied it up using pure stevia for the sweetener (about 2 tsps) and a few drops of lemon extract.  Huge success.  In a 7" spring form pan, cooked in air fryer for 45 minutes  to get to 150 degrees.  Delicious!

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
I’m a 40  year old new dad, who baked something last time… maybe a decade ago? Well, I’m a different man now, but my sweet tooth remains. I would never in my wildest dreams think I could make a cheesecake. Nor would I think I could make it again and again and again. But here we are and this recipe is *so* easy-to-follow and most importantly FAST to execute. Yes, the cheesecake needs to cool (duh) but I can let this bake in my trusty air fryer and tackle some other household choir, or focus on telling my under-one-year old daughter how many mouth-watering cheesecakes await in her future. Smacznego, córuś! 

P.S. I ever tried to make with plant-based ingredients, and… it tasted okay but visually a no-no.  No matter how long it stayed in the fridge it had a pudding-like consistency.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: You can, but only if you have a "true" or "European" convection oven.  Most ovens sold in the US are NOT true convection ovens,  They have only two heating elements, whereas true convection ovens add a third element, mounted near the fan, which heats the air as it is circulated directly from the fan.  I have owned both kinds and can attest that only true convection yields results equivalent to an air fryer.

author: Christina Morales
recipeTitle: Mexican Buñuelos With Piloncillo Syrup Recipe
commentBody:
I haven’t had traditional bunuelos - most definitely with the guava syrup - in SO LONG!  

I spent my childhood in Mexico City and remember one disastrous trip back to the States for Christmas to visit family (and introduce them to this amazing delicacy) when the jar of syrup broke in our suitcase!  My mom used my favorite pink sweater to try to mop up the mess up right there in the middle of baggage check!  I cried and cried, not for my sweater, but for the syrup!  

Thanks for the recipe!

author: Christina Morales
commentBody: I made this just like the recipe, but with one change. I omitted the strawberry preserves (I'm not too fond of those tiny seeds) and added cherry preserves, which added a bit of tartness. Everyone liked it. The next time I made it, I put a slab of ham on there with the turkey, which is a more traditional Cuban sandwich, and it was even better. For the guy who said he disapproved of changing recipes, well, here you go.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
When in Miami I try to stop in the Versailles Restaurant if I have time but would never think to try their Elena Ruz Sandwich which I note is priced at $7.50. 
Since I was going to ALDI today I got their brioche hot dog buns (6 ea), thin sliced turkey breast, strawberry preserves and whipped cream cheese and made one of these sandwiches.  It was really tasty and will add it to my regular sandwich list. I spent more than $7.50 but have enough to make at least 6 sandwiches.

commentBody: Thank you for the cranberry sauce suggestion! I like this with tunafish. Don't knock it til you try it. You know when you're at a wedding and they have those sandwiches with the crusts cut off? And everyone's like "is this turkey or chicken?" or "is this chicken or tuna?" No one can tell the difference, that's why cranberry sauce goes with all three! But the best of all is tunafish and cranberry sauce. btw (by the way), they have strawberry cream cheese too. I bet that would be great.

author: Christina Morales
commentBody:
I made this as written on brioche sliders side by side with ones where I subbed jellied cranberry sauce. Even with just a little strawberry jam we found the original too sweet. The combo was a little jarring at first bite which was surprising because I love salty/sweet. To be fair, I find most jams too sweet for my taste so I'm sure it's me.
Not to say I'd never have it again but I've loved the cranberry version for years so that's probably going to be my first choice. Thank you for sharing.
